course_description:
This course provides you with a multi-disciplinary perspective on ethical mental health practice. You will develop broad knowledge of the legal requirements, professional codes of practice and regulation that governs relevant professional disciplines, as well as their founding principles. You will explore systemic and personal issues that can impact the provision of ethical mental health practice, such as cultural, societal, organisational and personal value positions. Additional ethical models for mental health practice will be presented including consumer driven care, recovery models, and Indigenous Australian perspectives. The second part of this course will focus on the practical application of an ethical decision-making framework. Different aspects of ethical practice will be examined including therapist boundaries and behaviour, scope of practice and competency issues, informed consent and decisional capacity, risk management, confidentiality and the use of technology. You will explore complex ethical dilemmas with fellow students and others from different professional disciplines to promote a shared understanding of values and ethical perspectives. There will be the opportunity to engage in active communication and debate, as well as develop ethical safeguards specific to your personal mental health practice.

This foundation course will provide a firm base for further study and professional practice in mental health, psychotherapy and art therapy. Ethical and moral challenges are central in any mental health encounter. We will engage with the personal, interpersonal, organisational and regulatory components of working in mental health. In workshops you will actively engage with complex ethical issues with the intention of developing ethical intelligence in mental health practice. ﾠThere will be the opportunity to critically examine ethical practice, encourage debate and sharing of experiences and ideas. ﾠWe look forward to working with you.

UQ Public Timetable

Additional timetable information
TUTORIALS
These will be weekly 1.15-hour tutorials with experienced clinical professional tutors leading a group of students through some important topics and questions, supported by readings drawn from clinical and research literature. ﾠSee the UQ reading list for required and recommended resources.
The
Art Therapy
field of study will have 2 tutorials via zoom, one for remote students and a second for local students:
Art Therapy tutorial for remote students will be on Tuesdays 5:45 to 7pm with Tanya Burkhardt (Zoom link to be shared)
Art Therapy tutorial for local students will be on Wednesdays 5:30 to 6:45pm with Jane O'Sullivan (Zoom link to be shared)
Students from the
Psychotherapy Group
(includes Psychotherapy field of study, Graduate Certificate and students from other programs) will attend an evening tutorial via Zoom.
Psychotherapy tutorial will be on Wednesdays 5:30 to 6:45pm with Andrew Munnings (Zoom link to be shared)
WORKSHOPS
There will be compulsory in-person workshops
at Herston, Brisbane for PXMH7102. These will be activity focussed and assist you in engaging in a personal and interactive way with the key concepts in the course.
Monday 9 March will focus on orienting you to studying in the Post graduate Mental Health Program in the Medical School.
On Wednesday 11 March we will focus on learning activities: an Ethical Tribunal and Ethical Mental Health Practice with Priority Populations: An Indigenous Lens, with guest lecturer, Stacey Vervoort.
These workshops are required for you to attend. If you are unable to attend these workshops you need to contact the course coordinator. We look forwardﾠto working with you.
course_aims: This course aims to provide students with knowledge and experiences that will encourage ethical, sustainable and effective mental health practice.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically engage with consumer and carer perspectives in mental health practice.
Analyse the regulatory and professional structures governing mental health practice in Australia, including emerging ethical challenges related to digital and AI-mediated practice.
Analyse and apply ethical principles to dilemmas and challenges in mental health practice.
Develop and defend ethically sound systems, policies, and safeguards in mental health practice.

assessment_summary.title: Ethical Decision-Making Framework: Application & Critical Reflection Essay

assessment_details.title: The Consumer Experience
assessment_details.task_description: Students will complete a written report based on an interview with a consumer or carer who has engaged with mental health, psychotherapy, counselling, or art therapy services. The purpose of the task is to explore real experiences of care, including examples of both respectful/effective and less helpful practices, and to reflect on the implications for ethical, person-centred practice. The report must demonstrate sensitive engagement with the interviewee, integrate their narrative meaningfully, and include a structured personal reflection on the learning gained from the interview process. Students must attach a signed consent form in Appendix A, confirming the interviewee understood the purpose of the interview and agreed to participate. Students must also include original interview notes in Appendix B, in order to support academic integrity and authorship. Word limit: 1,500 words (+/- 10%) Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Ethical Decision-Making Framework: Application & Critical Reflection Essay
assessment_details.task_description: Length: 2,500 words (+/- 10%, excluding appendix) Students will prepare a written essay in two parts that develops an individual ethical decision-making framework for mental health practice and critically reflects on the use of this framework in conjunction with generative artificial intelligence (AI). The task is designed to deepen students’ understanding of ethical theory, professional values, and real-world decision-making processes, while also encouraging critical evaluation of the emerging role of AI in mental health practice. PART 1: Ethical Framework Development Students will conduct a literature review drawing on ethical theory, professional codes, regulatory expectations, and relevant mental health ethics literature. Using this evidence base, students will develop and justify a personalised ethical decision-making framework tailored to their professional identity and therapeutic context. The framework should be original, theoretically supported, and structured as a practical step-by-step model for guiding future ethical decisions in mental health practice. PART 2: Applied Reflection & AI Analysis Students will apply their ethical framework to a hypothetical ethical dilemma provided in the course and will input their framework into a generative AI tool to explore how AI interprets, applies, or distorts their model. Students will then critically analyse the strengths, limitations, and ethical risks associated with AI-mediated decision making, including potential issues around bias, confidentiality, professional judgement, clinical nuance, and client safety. Students will conclude this section with a reflective statement articulating their professional position on whether, when, and how AI could be ethically incorporated into mental health practice. Appendix Requirement: All AI prompts and AI-generated outputs must be submitted in an appendix. Students must clearly distinguish their own ideas, interpretation, and reasoning within the essay from any AI-generated material. Failure to include prompts/output may result in academic penalty. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Ethical Practice Tutorial
assessment_details.task_description: Across Weeks 7–10, students will participate in Ethical Practice Tutorials designed to explore how ethical principles translate into real-world mental health work. Each student will be assigned to one tutorial week in which they will contribute to the teaching process by presenting a short, de-identified practice case example that illustrates the ethical theme for that week. For their allocated week, students will briefly outline the case, highlight the relevant ethical principles drawn from the assigned readings, and engage in a tutor-led Q&A discussion to demonstrate understanding and application of ethical ideas. While only the allocated week is formally assessed, all students are encouraged to contribute to discussions across the tutorial series to support an interactive and collegial learning environment. Assessment will consider: • the clarity and relevance of the case example to the ethical theme • demonstrated integration of core readings and ethical theory • ability to engage with questions and discussion in their allocated week Confidentiality requirement: All case material must be fully de-identified and free of information that could reasonably identify clients, settings, or organisations. Composite cases may be used where necessary to protect confidentiality. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submission will occur during an assigned tutorial time, to be discussed with your tutor.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Those who are unable to attend and have an approved extension will be provided with a date for an oral examination outside of tutorial times.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension .

assessment_details.title: Ethical Safeguards
assessment_details.task_description: Ethical mental health practice requires proactive planning and preventative safeguards to reduce the likelihood of future ethical dilemmas. For this assessment students will design an original ethical safeguard (e.g., clinical guideline, policy or structured protocol) relevant to their area of mental health practice. The safeguard should reflect the student's own original thinking and be informed by relevant ethical theory, professional codes and research evidence. Students will present their safeguard to the class in a brief 10-minute presentation , outlining: the ethical issue or context the safeguard addresses, the rationale underpinning their guideline or policy, and important limitations, caveats, and implementation considerations. Following the presentation, students will engage in a 5-minute Q&A , responding to questions about the conceptual basis, applicability, and ethical reasoning underpinning their safeguard. The Q&A is designed to strengthen authorship security and give students the opportunity to demonstrate genuine understanding of the ethical and practical implications of their proposed safeguard. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to PXMH7102 You will be oriented to the learning activities of PXMH7102, including relevant resources, assessment and expectations. You will have the opportunity to meet your tutor and fellow students and reflect on the following questions: 1. Why am I studying a degree in Mental Health? 2. What are the professional, ethical, personal questions I want addressed?

Ethical Care: Developing a Consumer Focus You will explore what constitutes ethical care through the lens of the consumer. You will critically reflect on the mental health system in Australia and whether it delivers consumer focused care. Guidance on the first assessment piece will be provided. Learning outcomes: L01

Workshop Week- Program Student Orientation The Student Orientation will be held on Monday 09/03/2026. First year students are encouraged to attend this orientation which will provide guidance on successful study at UQ.

Workshop Week- Ethical Violations: Ethics Tribunal Ethics Tribunal will be held on Wednesday, 11/03/2026 You will engage in an ethical tribunal which will highlight key ethical issues that need to be considered and the impact of ethical violations. You should develop an understanding of: - How are professionals involved in mental health and psychotherapy in Australia trained, structured and regulated? - What is the role and process of professional registration and discipline procedures? What are the outcomes of ethics tribunals? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Workshop Week- Ethical Practice with Priority Populations The Ethical Practice with Priority Population lecture will be held on Wednesday 11/03/2026. You will explore Ethical Mental Health Practice using an Indigenous Australian Lens, with guest lecturer Stacey Vervoort. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Ethical Principles and Professionalism You will develop understanding of: -ethical principles underlying mental health practice and professions -common characteristics of ethics codes for professions in mental health and psychotherapy -the landscape of mental health care in Australia and critically analyse whether mental health services have the capacity to deliver ethical care -personal values or other factors that can be integrated into your ethical mental health practice. Learning outcomes: L02

Ethical Decision Making: Developing and testing a Framework You will have the opportunity to: -to identify and define ethical dilemmas -develop a strategy of response to ethical dilemmas -Account for societal, legal, organisational and personal influences on ethical decision making Learning outcomes: L02
Mental Health Ethics in the age of AI The evolving practice of psychotherapy and art therapy will be explored and the unique emerging ethical issues that technology presents. Learning outcomes: L02

Ethical Practice Series: The Psychotherapy Relationship You will explore the psychotherapy relationship, how to define it and pertinent ethical issues such as, dual relationships, boundary crossings and violations. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Ethical Practice Series: Client Rights You will explore how to maintain client rights (such as client dignity and autonomy) during the therapeutic process. The specific ethical issues of confidentiality and privacy, and informed consent and decisional capacity will be examined. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Ethical Practice: High Risk Situations You will be able to identify high risk situations that can emerge in mental health practice. The ethical challenges that arise in this context will be explored including: duty to care, duty to warn, using power ethically. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Ethical Practice: Professional Integrity Understand how to practice within the bounds of competence, and the importance of therapist self care and determining fitness to practice. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Presentations: Ethical Safeguards You will have the opportunity to learn from their colleagues. Specific learning will be focused on: -recognising the complexity and skills required for working in diverse mental health settings -identifying pertinent ethical issues in specific mental health settings - developing an understanding of a range of mental health practices designed to prevent the emergence of ethical dilemmas. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Presentations: Ethical Safeguards cont. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Presentations: Ethical Safeguards cont. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
